if-decons-expr.summary: Checks if first argument is non empty expression.
if-decons-expr.description: Checks if first argument is non empty expression. If so gets tail and head from the first argument and returns forth argument using head and tail values. Returns fifth argument otherwise.

import!.summary: Imports module using its relative path (second argument), which could contain ':' as a path separation (e.g.
import!.description: Imports module using its relative path (second argument), which could contain ':' as a path separation (e.g. !(import &module relative:path:to:module)) and binds it to the token (first argument) which will represent imported atomspace. If first argument is &self then everything will be imported to current atomspace)

params.description: Symbol, which is turned into the token for accessing the imported module
Symbol, which is turned into the token for accessing the imported module

overloads.description: Imports module using its relative path (second argument), which could contain ':' as a path separation (e.g. !(import &module relative:path:to:module)) and binds it to the token (first argument) which will represent imported atomspace. If first argument is &self then everything will be imported to current atomspace)

include.description: Works just like import! but with &self as a first argument. So everything from input file will be included in the current atomspace and evaluated

Name of metta script to import

intersection.summary: Function takes two non-deterministic inputs (first and second argument) and returns their intersection.
intersection.description: Function takes two non-deterministic inputs (first and second argument) and returns their intersection. E.g. (intersection (superpose (a b c c)) (superpose (b c c c d))) -> [b, c, c]

examples.eval: !(intersection (superpose (a b c c)) (superpose (b c c c d)))

overloads.description: Function takes two non-deterministic inputs (first and second argument) and returns their intersection. E.g. (intersection (superpose (a b c c)) (superpose (b c c c d))) -> [b, c, c]

intersection-atom.description: Function takes two tuples and returns their intersection. E.g. (intersection-atom (a b c c) (b c c c d)) -> (b c c)

examples.eval: !(intersection-atom (a b c c) (b c c c d))

Expression which will be evaluated if each pair can be unified

map-atom.summary: Function takes list of atoms (first argument), variable to be used inside (second variable) and an expression which will be evaluated for each atom in list (third argument).
map-atom.description: Function takes list of atoms (first argument), variable to be used inside (second variable) and an expression which will be evaluated for each atom in list (third argument). Expression should contain variable. So e.g. (map-atom (1 2 3 4) $v (eval (+ $v 1))) will give (2 3 4 5)

Result of evaluating template for each atom in a list

pragma!.summary: Changes global key's (first argument) value to a new one (second argument).
pragma!.description: Changes global key's (first argument) value to a new one (second argument). Possible pragmas: (pragma! type-check auto) - check type of the atom before evaluation (pragma! interpreter bare-minimal) - use minimal MeTTa semantics when evaluating atom (pragma! max-stack-depth ) - limit depth of the interpreter's stack, 0 - no limit (default behavior)

overloads.description: Changes global key's (first argument) value to a new one (second argument). Possible pragmas: (pragma! type-check auto) - check type of the atom before evaluation (pragma! interpreter bare-minimal) - use minimal MeTTa semantics when evaluating atom (pragma! max-stack-depth ) - limit depth of the interpreter's stack, 0 - no limit (default behavior)
